era_context: The Axial Age in full development. Neo-Babylonian Empire consolidating control over the Near East. Chinese Spring and Autumn period fragmentation ongoing. Greek philosophy and democracy emerging. South Asian Vedic-to-Upanishadic transition. Iron Age warfare and technology spreading across Eurasia. Major intellectual and political ferment across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (limited contact with literate civilisations)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written sources)

events.title: Jehoiachin deported to Babylon; Zedekiah installed as vassal king
events.description: Following the siege of Jerusalem (dated to either 598 or 597 BCE, depending on the source), Jehoiachin is deported to Babylon along with the Judean elite, including the prophet Ezekiel. Nebuchadnezzar II installs Zedekiah (Jehoiachin's uncle) as a vassal king to rule Judah on Babylon's behalf. This event marks the beginning of the Babylonian exile, a transformative period in Jewish history that profoundly influences the development of Judaism, including the composition of biblical texts and the emergence of rabbinic tradition. The deportation is attested in both Babylonian and Hebrew sources.

sources.name: Book of Ezekiel (biblical text, composed during or after the exile)
Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (1st century CE)

events.certainty_note: The deportation of Jehoiachin and installation of Zedekiah are well-attested in both Babylonian and Hebrew sources. The dating (597 or 598 BCE) is debated, but the event itself is confirmed. Most modern scholars favour 597 BCE for the deportation, with the siege beginning in 598 BCE.

events.title: Lao Tzu (Laozi) traditionally dated to this era
events.description: According to traditional Chinese sources, Lao Tzu (Laozi, 老子), the legendary founder of Daoism, lived during the Spring and Autumn period. Some sources place his birth around 604 BCE or his floruit around 597 BCE. However, modern scholarship is deeply sceptical of these dates; Laozi may be a legendary or composite figure, and the Daodejing (Tao Te Ching) attributed to him was likely composed much later, possibly during the Warring States period (5th–3rd century BCE) or even later. The traditional dating is included here for cultural significance, but the historical reality is highly uncertain.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, 1st century BCE
Daodejing (Tao Te Ching), authorship and dating disputed

events.certainty_note: Laozi's historical existence is disputed by modern scholars. The traditional dating to the Spring and Autumn period (including 597 BCE) is found in Sima Qian's Shiji, but this source is much later (1st century BCE) and may conflate or mythologise earlier figures. The Daodejing is likely a later compilation, possibly from the 4th–3rd century BCE or later.

events.title: Spring and Autumn period warfare in China continues
events.description: The fragmentation of the Zhou Dynasty continues, with competing states (Qi, Jin, Chu, Qin, Song, and others) engaging in constant warfare and diplomatic manoeuvring. The Spring and Autumn Annals (Chunqiu) records various military campaigns and political events, though the entries are often terse. The year 597 BCE is attested in Chinese chronicles, but specific events are not well-documented in surviving sources. The broader pattern of state competition and the emergence of powerful regional lords (ba) characterises this period.

sources.name: Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals), traditional chronicle
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn period is well-attested in Chinese chronicles. Specific events of 597 BCE are recorded in the Chunqiu but are often terse and lack detail. The dating is secure within the framework of Chinese chronology, though absolute dating to the proleptic Gregorian calendar has some uncertainty (±1–2 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Jehoiachin's deportation is disputed between 598 and 597 BCE, depending on the source and calendar system used. Modern scholars are divided, though 597 BCE is increasingly favoured. Laozi's historical existence is highly disputed; modern scholarship suggests he may be legendary or a composite figure, and the Daodejing is likely a later compilation. The traditional dating of Laozi to the Spring and Autumn period is rejected by many scholars as unreliable.
historiographic_note: The year 597 BCE is documented primarily through the lens of the Babylonian conquest of Judah. Babylonian cuneiform records provide contemporary documentation, though terse. Hebrew biblical sources provide narrative detail but are not contemporary and reflect theological interpretation. Chinese chronicles (Chunqiu) record the year but provide minimal detail. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Near East and China; other regions are absent from written sources. The dating of events in this year is subject to calendar conversion uncertainties, particularly for the Babylonian and Hebrew chronologies.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Jerusalem (598 BCE) leads to Jehoiachin's deportation (597 BCE)
Jehoiachin's deportation is part of the broader Babylonian conquest of Judah
The Babylonian exile (beginning 597 BCE) ends with the return under Cyrus II (539 BCE)
Laozi and Confucius are traditionally dated to the same era (Spring and Autumn period)
